pages.text: describes the procedure (mixing and casting) used for the preparation of mortars. They were cast in 2 /C22 /C216 cm prisms and demolded 24 h after casting. The prisms were then stored at 60 /C176C, after being placed on grids in watertight contain- ers containing 20 mm of water (mortar bars were not in contact with the water). Expansion was measured using the scale micrometer method (specimens had shrinkage bolts at both ends). Each measurement was the mean of three values from three replicate specimens. Expansion measurements were performed after the containers and the prisms had been cooled for 24 h at 20 /C176C. This test is based on the standard NF P 18-454 [4] and was designed for concrete, but it has been pre- viously validated on mortars [5] and on specimens of size 2 /C22 /C216 cm [6,7]. Moreover, experiments have also shown that comparable results were obtained with specimen size of 4 /C24 /C216 cm and 2 /C22 /C216 cm. The ﬁrst part of the study consisted in evaluating the swelling potential due to alkali–silica reaction of all size classes of glass. Thus 20% of non-reactive sand was replaced in each mortar by glass of equivalent ﬁneness (samples M i, where i is the class of glass). Grading curves were adapted when the tested glass class was out of the range of non-reactive sand (160–2500 lm). In the second part of the study, the efﬁciency of ﬁne glass particles to counteract the ASR of coarser ones (C0 and C1) was tested, by using three classes of ﬁnes (C5, C6 and C8) as 20% and 40% replace- ment of non-reactive sand. These samples were noted C i– X%Ck (i and k: classes of glass; i = 0, 1 and k = 5 ,6 ,8 ; X replacement rate of non-reactive sand = 20% and 40%). Extractions of pore ﬂuid were carried out on mortars M1 (coarse aggregate C1) and C1–20% C8, C1–40% C8 (coarse aggregate C1 with 20% and 40% of ﬁne C8), according to the procedure given by Cyr et al. [8]. 3. Results 3.1. The different classes of glass tested separately Results of expansion measurements on mortar prisms made with 20% of glass of different classes (replacement of non-reactive sand) are given in Fig. 1. It can be seen that the particle size of the glass is a major parameter controlling the expansion values. A crit- ical threshold of grain size around 0.9–1 mm was observed, under which no expansion occurred. Only coarse particle size classes (2.5–5 mm and 1.25–2.5 mm) led to signiﬁcant expansion of mor- tar prisms. Particle sizes under that threshold had no effect on length variations. Some authors have already evoked the existence of a critical diameter causing abnormal expansion due to ASR. Jin et al. [9] and Xie et al. [10] estimated this diameter to be between 0.60 and 1.18 mm. However, other authors have found lower val- ues, e.g. between 0.15 and 0.30 for Yamada and Ishiyama [11]. In the actual conditions of the test, glass particle sizes under 1 mm were not deleterious in terms of alkali–silica reaction (ASR). In order to evaluate the consequences of this result on the mechanical behaviour of the mortars, compressive strength tests were carried out on all mixtures. Fig. 1 also gives the compressive strength results of mortars containing the different size classes of glass and cured in the same conditions as for ASR tests. It can be seen that, compared to reference mortar without glass, the strengths were up to 10 MPa lower when alkali-reactive classes C0 and C1 were used. In contrast, ﬁnes of classes C3–C8 systemat- ically gave extra strength, which

4. Discussion It has been suggested that the deleterious or beneﬁcial activity of a material towards ASR depends on its particle size [12]. Thus glass powders and typical pozzolans, such as ﬂy ash or silica fumes, could probably be classiﬁed in the same category and their activity as ASR-reducers might be explained using the same mechanisms as proposed for pozzolans. Several mechanisms have been put for- ward to explain the effectiveness of pozzolans against ASR: – A dilution of alkalis in concrete when low-alkali pozzolans are used as cement replacement [13]. This hypothesis cannot explain the results since glass brings a signiﬁcant amount of alkalis (13.9% Na 2Oeq), leading to higher alkali contents in the mixtures. – Accommodation of the gel by the porosity of the cement paste for particles under a given size; this hypothesis, which is the basis of several numerical models, states that the porosity of the cement paste is able to accommodate the gels formed (what- ever their nature) without causing expansion. In fact, it has been shown [14] that, depending on the size of reactive grains, the gels could not induce ASR-swelling. In this case, the porosity of the paste is high enough so that the gel has enough space to diffuse into the paste, without exerting excessive stress on the solid skeleton and cause swelling. Therefore, this explana- tion remains valid only when ﬁnes and aggregate of glass are used separately, but it does not explain how ﬁnes reduce swell- ing when the amount of coarse aggregates remains constant. For constant content of coarse particle, it is shown (Fig. 2 ) that increasing amounts of ﬁnes can limit the expansion. So this hypothesis cannot be taken into account here and another mechanism must explain the results obtained. – A decrease in the permeability of the concrete and thus in the ionic mobility, leading to a reduction of the migration of alkalis towards the reactive aggregate [15–17]. In fact, the use of glass ﬁnes leads to the formation of C–S–H by the mean of pozzolanic reaction. These C–S–H increase the density of the structure of cement paste and lead to a decrease of the porosity and the per- meability. At the same time these C–S–H lead to an improve- ment in the strength of the cement paste and a consequently higher resistance to the expansive stresses due to ASR-gels [16,17]. Fig. 4 shows that the use of ﬁnes systematically led to a signiﬁcant increase of strength and a decrease of the expan- sion, thus conﬁrming the beneﬁcial effect of the pozzolanic reaction. – A production of pozzolanic C–S–H of low C/S ratio, which have the capacity to absorb a signiﬁcantly higher quantity of alkalis than normal C–S–H, thus reducing the quantity of alkali ions and the pH in the pore solution [13,15,17,18]. Our extraction tests of the pore solution (Fig. 5 ) show that the use of glass pow- der C8 in mortars to counteract the ASR of coarse glass particles (C1) involved a signiﬁcant increase of total alkali content, from 64 mmol of Na eq/g of mortar (without C8) to 1227 mmol/g (with 40% C8). Although the fraction of alkalis in solution tend to decrease when increasing the quantity of C8 in the mixtures (probably meaning that pozzolanic C–S–H absorb more alkalis than normal C–S–H), the concentration of alkali in the solution remained four times higher with 40% C8. Technical Note Use of ﬁne glass as ASR inhibitor in glass aggregate mortars Rachida Idir a,b, Martin Cyr a,*, Arezki Tagnit-Hamou b a Laboratoire Matériaux et Durabilité des Constructions (LMDC), Université de Toulouse, INSA-UPS, Génie Civil, 135, avenue de Rangueil, 31077 Toulouse Cedex, France b Département de Génie Civil, Université de Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke (Québec), Canada J1K 2R1 article info Article history: Received 25 October 2009 Received in revised form 5 December 2009 Accepted 16 December 2009 Available online 13 January 2010 Keywords: Waste-glass Recycling Glass aggregate Glass powder Alkali–silica reaction Pozzolanic reaction Mortar abstract Demand for recycled glass has considerably decreased in recent years, particularly for mixed glass. Glass is cheaper to store than to recycle, as conditioners require expenses for the recycling process. In order to provide a sustainable solution to glass storage, a potential and incentive way would be to reuse this type of glass in concretes. Depending on the size of the glass particles used in concrete, two antagonistic behaviours can be observed: alkali–silica reaction, which involves negative effects, and pozzolanic reac- tion, improving the properties of concrete. The work undertaken here dealt with the use of ﬁne particles of glass and glass aggregates in mortars, either separately or combined. Two parameters based on stand- ardised tests were studied: pozzolanic assessment by mechanical tests on mortar samples and alkali- reactive aggregate characteristics and ﬁnes inhibitor evaluations by monitoring of dimensional changes. It is shown that there is no need to use glass in the form of ﬁnes since no swelling due to alkali–silica reaction is recorded when the diameter of the glass grains is less than 1 mm. Besides, ﬁne glass powders having speciﬁc surface areas ranging from 180 to 540 m 2/kg reduce the expansions of mortars subjected to alkali–silica reaction (especially when glass aggregates of diameters larger than 1 mm are used). /C2112009 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Glass is a common product that can be found in different forms: bottles, jars, windows and windshields, bulbs, cathode ray tubes, etc. These products have a limited lifetime and must be recycled in order to avoid environmental problems related to their stockpil- ing or landﬁlling. Several recycling channels already exist for glass recovery, the main one being the manufacture of new glass prod- ucts. This operation is usually feasible when the glass is recovered as separate colours to produce glass products of the same colour. However, most of the time, the collected glass is mixed and so unusable for the production of bottles of a given colour. Conse- quently, secondary uses for recycled glass have been developed such as abrasive, glass wool or water ﬁltration media. Another important channel for the recycling of mixed glass is cement-based materials, but most of existing studies recommend its use only as ﬁne powders [1,2]. Fine particles of glass usually present pozzola- nic activity beneﬁcial to the concrete, while coarse particles are usually deleterious to concrete due to alkali–silica reaction (ASR). Although the use of ﬁne particles is an effective solution for glass in concrete, the crushing of glass represents a signiﬁcant cost since several hours of treatment are needed to obtain an efﬁcient ﬁneness of glass (almost equivalent to cement). The aim of this study is to recycle glass in c

expansions were obtained when the alkali concentrations were higher, it means that the efﬁciency of ﬁne glass particles cannot be due only to alkali trapping by C–S–H. Other assumptions than removal of alkali hydroxides from solution are found to explain the efﬁciency of ﬁne glass powder. Helmuth et al. [19] suggested that factors related to the excess of alkalis can explain the reduction of expansions, for instance: activated reaction of the reactive particles, which soften the particles to relieve the expansive pressure; rapid reaction leading to diffusion of silica to unconﬁned sites; high Na/Si ratios favouring the decrease of the gel viscosity and so the swelling pressure. At very high alkali content, the attacked silica would not be able to polymerize. Works are still in progress to verify these last hypotheses. 5. Conclusion This study aimed to evaluate the preventive role of pozzolanic glass ﬁnes in counteracting the deleterious effect of alkali-reactive glass aggregates. It has been shown that the use of both types of glass particles is pertinent. The main results were that: – Only glass classes of more that 1 mm gave expansions related to alkali–silica reaction. – The use of glass ﬁnes led to the reduction of mortar expansion due to coarse particles; moreover, ﬁnes increased the compres- sive strength of mortars. – No excessive crushing of glass ﬁnes was needed since the quantity of ﬁnes was the main parameter controlling the reduction of expansion due to coarse glass aggregates. It is thus preferable to use 40% of class C5 (d 50 of 120 lm) rather than 20% of class C8 (d 50 of 8 lm). These results lead to eco- nomic beneﬁt: more glass can be used and the crushing time required can be limited.
